sn22.59:1.1: I have heard that on one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Vārāṇasī in the Deer Park at Isipatana.
sn22.59:1.2: There he addressed the group of five monks:
sn22.59:2.2: If form were the self, this form would not lend itself to dis-ease. It would be possible (to say) with regard to form,
sn22.59:2.3: ‘Let my form be thus. Let my form not be thus.’
sn22.59:2.4: But precisely because form is not self, this form lends itself to dis-ease.
sn22.59:2.5: And it is not possible (to say) with regard to form, ‘Let my form be thus. Let my form not be thus.’
sn22.59:5.2: If consciousness were the self, this consciousness would not lend itself to dis-ease. It would be possible (to say) with regard to consciousness,
sn22.59:5.3: ‘Let my consciousness be thus. Let my consciousness not be thus.’
sn22.59:5.4: But precisely because consciousness is not self, consciousness lends itself to dis-ease. And it is not possible (to say) with regard to consciousness,
sn22.59:5.5: ‘Let my consciousness be thus. Let my consciousness not be thus.’
sn22.59:6.4: “And is that which is inconstant easeful or stressful?”
sn22.59:6.6: “And is it fitting to regard what is inconstant, stressful, subject to change as:
sn22.59:6.7: ‘This is mine. This is my self. This is what I am’?”
sn22.59:6.9: “… Is feeling constant or inconstant?” — “Inconstant, lord.” …
sn22.59:6.10: “… Is perception constant or inconstant?” — “Inconstant, lord.” …
sn22.59:6.11: “… Are fabrications constant or inconstant?” — “Inconstant, lord.” …
sn22.59:6.12: “What do you think, monks? Is consciousness constant or inconstant?”
sn22.59:6.14: “And is that which is inconstant easeful or stressful?”
sn22.59:6.16: “And is it fitting to regard what is inconstant, stressful, subject to change as:
sn22.59:6.17: ‘This is mine. This is my self. This is what I am’?”
sn22.59:7.1: “Thus, monks, any form whatsoever that is past, future, or present; internal or external; blatant or subtle; common or sublime; far or near: Every1 form is to be seen with right discernment as it has come to be: ‘This is not mine. This is not my self. This is not what I am.’
sn22.59:10.1: Any consciousness whatsoever that is past, future, or present; internal or external; blatant or subtle; common or sublime; far or near: Every consciousness is to be seen with right discernment as it has come to be: ‘This is not mine. This is not my self. This is not what I am.’
sn22.59:11.1: Seeing thus, the instructed disciple of the noble ones grows disenchanted with form, disenchanted with feeling, disenchanted with perception, disenchanted with fabrications, disenchanted with consciousness.
sn22.59:11.2: Disenchanted, he becomes dispassionate. Through dispassion, he is released.
sn22.59:11.3: With release, there is the knowledge, ‘Released.’ He discerns that ‘Birth is ended, the holy life fulfilled, the task done. There is nothing further for this world.’”
sn22.59:12.1: That is what the Blessed One said.
sn22.59:12.2: Gratified, the group of five monks delighted in the Blessed One’s words.
sn22.59:12.3: And while this explanation was being given, the minds of the group of five monks, through lack of clinging/sustenance, were released from effluents.
